coredata.dc:title: Effect of polymer–graphene-quantum-dot solution on enhanced oil recovery performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zahiri, Mohammad Ghader
Esmaeilnezhad, Ehsan
Choi, Hyoung Jin
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to utilize graphene-quantum-dots in addition to a D118 superpusher polymer to increase oil recovery from mature oil fields. First, nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized and fully characterized. These were then added to polymer solutions so the prepared fluids could alter the microscopic and macroscopic sweep efficiencies. To identify the best samples, concentrations of the NPs and polymer, as well as two types of salt, were carefully selected as influencing parameters using Design Expert as a time- and cost-saving technique. Interfacial tension (IFT) and contact angle—parameters affecting microscopic efficiency—as well as shear viscosity, which affects macroscopic efficiency, were considered as responses in this method. IFT reduction (of approximately 1.78 mN/m) and an extraordinary change in the wettability to hydrophilic were achieved, particularly in carbonate rocks as opposed to sandstone. In addition, the polymer resulted in improved mobility ratio. From the experiment and analysis of variance, two flooding fluids that increase the recovery factors for carbonate and sandstone core samples by up to 31% and 20%, respectively, are proposed. Furthermore, core flooding experiments confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed fluids for enhanced oil recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enhanced oil recovery
Graphene quantum dot
Interfacial tension
Design expert

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NP synthesis and polymer–NP solution preparation
Cores and slabs: preparation and characterization
IFT, contact angle, and viscosity measurements
Selection procedure of the proposed sample for EOR

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of core flooding setup: (1) brine, (2) oil, (3) proposed fluid, (4) hydraulic oil, (5) accumulator, (6) bypass valve, (7) confining pressure, (8) confining sleeve, (9) oven, (10) pump controller, (11) pressure gauge, and (12) outlet.
(a) Viscosity and (b) shear stress vs. shear rate of polymer–NP solutions in low- (open) and high- (closed) salinity water at the NP concentration of 0.5 wt% and different polymer concentrations.
(a) Viscosity and (b) shear stress vs. shear rate of polymer–NP solutions in low (open) and high (closed) saline water at the NP concentration of 1 wt% and different concentrations of polymer.
(a) Shear viscosity and (b) shear stress vs. shear rate of polymer–NP solution in low- (open) and high- (closed) salinity water at an NP concentration of 1.5 wt% and different polymer concentrations.
IFT test results at different concentrations of D118 polymer in low (open) and high (closed) salinity.
Contact angles of (a) carbonate and (b) sandstone slabs at different concentrations of D118 polymer solutions in low (open) and high (closed) salinity.
Pressure drop (open) and recovery factor (closed) in carbonate core (core B). Proposed fluid: GQDs (0.5 wt%) and polymer (2000 ppm) solution at low salinity.
Pressure drop (open) and recovery factor (closed) in carbonate core (core C). Proposed fluid: GQDs (0.5 wt%) and polymer (2000 ppm) solution at high salinity.
Pressure drop (open) and recovery factor (closed) in sandstone core (core A). Proposed fluid: GQDs (0.5 wt%) and polymer (2000 ppm) solution at high salinity.

head.ce:title: Effect of polymer–graphene-quantum-dot solution on enhanced oil recovery performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Petroleum Engineering, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Petroleum Engineering, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Program of Environmental and Polymer Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Program of Environmental and Polymer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Program of Environmental and Polymer Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea (H.J. Choi).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to utilize graphene-quantum-dots in addition to a D118 superpusher polymer to increase oil recovery from mature oil fields. First, nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized and fully characterized. These were then added to polymer solutions so the prepared fluids could alter the microscopic and macroscopic sweep efficiencies. To identify the best samples, concentrations of the NPs and polymer, as well as two types of salt, were carefully selected as influencing parameters using Design Expert as a time- and cost-saving technique. Interfacial tension (IFT) and contact angle—parameters affecting microscopic efficiency—as well as shear viscosity, which affects macroscopic efficiency, were considered as responses in this method. IFT reduction (of approximately 1.78 mN/m) and an extraordinary change in the wettability to hydrophilic were achieved, particularly in carbonate rocks as opposed to sandstone. In addition, the polymer resulted in improved mobility ratio. From the experiment and analysis of variance, two flooding fluids that increase the recovery factors for carbonate and sandstone core samples by up to 31% and 20%, respectively, are proposed. Furthermore, core flooding experiments confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed fluids for enhanced oil recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, there has been a sharp decline in the exploration of new oil and gas reservoirs and in the total volume of oil extracted from reservoirs using natural and primary methods. This has spurred many oil companies to increase oil recovery from mature oil reservoirs to meet the demand for hydrocarbons [1,2]. Enhanced oil recovery (EOR) processes, including polymer nanocomposite flooding are employed to significantly improve the oil recovery factor [3,4]. Water flooding is the first candidate method, and the secondary recovery process can extract approximately 40% of the oil [5]. However, more than 60% of the initial oil cannot be recovered because of problems such as bypassing and improper sweeping of the oil during water flooding. To improve the efficiency of water flooding, materials such as nanoparticles (NPs), which alter the reservoir rock wettability and decrease the oil/water interfacial tension (IFT) [6,7], and polymers, which optimize the mobility ratio [8–10], are added to water, affecting the microscopic and macroscopic sweep efficiencies, respectively [11,12].

Polymer flooding is a widely used oil extraction method and an alternative to conventional flooding methods [13]. The most advanced nanotechnology in the oil and gas industry is applied to EOR [14,15]. Moreover, utilizing NPs is an effective way to increase the efficiency of polymer flooding in oil reservoirs [16]. However, for field-scale applications, the NP synthesis method should be easy and cost effective [17].

Partially hydrolyzed polyacrylamide (HPAM) and xanthan biopolymer are popularly utilized in EOR [18]. They are also used as thickeners in other industries [19]; however, they are sensitive to high salinity/temperature conditions and bacterial attack [20]. The formation water in hydrocarbon reservoirs contains significant amounts of divalent ions such as calcium and magnesium, which can eventually lead to a sharp drop in viscosity due to ion bridges [21]. Hence, associative polymers, such as D118, have been developed in recent years to alleviate these problems [18]. The functional groups of such polymers mainly comprise long hydrophilic chains. Owing to the creation of three-dimensional networks through molecular and intermolecular junctions, these polymers are less sensitive to salinity and temperature than conventional polymers [22,23]. Therefore, they increase the resistance of the polymer solutions under harsh conditions (high-temperature and high-salinity water) of the oil reservoir and can significantly increase the EOR performance [24]. The D118 superpusher polymer, compared with other conventional polymers in petroleum engineering such as HPAM, exhibits a high shear viscosity as well as the ability to increase the thickness by increasing the degree of hydrophobicity against increasing water salinity. Thus, D118 is one of the best candidates for polymer flooding because it not only creates good viscosity, but is also compatible with reservoir saltwater and maintains thermal stability over time [25].

Concurrently, silica NPs are the most commonly used NPs for EOR owing to their simple synthesis method [26,27]. Second in popularity are metal- and carbon-based NPs [28,29]. Many types of NPs, such as Fe3O4, Al2O3, TiO2, and multiwalled carbon nanotubes, are applicable to EOR [30]. A carbon-based NP that has recently been considered extensively is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which have low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and dispersity, high stability, and thermal conductivity properties. Their effectiveness in increasing oil recovery performance has been reported [31–33].

In this study, for the first time, the combined effects of an associate polymer and GQDs on wettability alteration and IFT reduction as well as the rheological properties regarding the mobility ratios on sandstone and carbonate rocks were fully investigated using experimental and statistical techniques. The oil recovery factor resulting from the proposed fluid flooding is reported herein.

Sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and citric acid (C6H8O7) were purchased from Merck Co. (Germany). A commercial associate polymer, superpusher D118 (hydrolysis degree: 25–30 mol% at 25 °C; appearance: white granular solid; total anion content: 15–25 mol%; hydrophobe content: medium [34], was obtained from SNF Floerger, France. Two types of rock, sandstone and carbonate, were obtained from the outcrop of two reservoirs in southwestern Iran, as well as crude oil (32.29 API @ 60 °F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NP synthesis and polymer–NP solution preparation
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared via a simple thermal process [35], in which 2 g of citric acid was first pulverized and then exposed directly to a temperature of 200 °C in an oven. After 5 min, the powder became colorless and gradually turned pale brown. After half an hour, the color changed to orange. To prepare the suspension, distilled water was added to the synthesized GQDs and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 30 min.

The API RP63 standard [36] was followed for the preparation of the polymer stock solution (4000 ppm), in which a vortex was first made in the distilled water using a stirrer (with high revolutions per minute). Then, the polymer powder was gradually added, after which the solution was released at low revolutions per minute overnight until the polymer solution became completely homogeneous, clear, and without any bubbles. To determine the concentrations of polymer, salt, and NP dispersion, the prepared polymer stock solution was diluted with saline water (low-salinity water: 42000 ppm NaCl, or high-salinity water: 42000 ppm NaCl + 18000 ppm CaCl2), and NP suspensions were prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cores and slabs: preparation and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of rock were cut using a core-cutting machine (Fars EOR Co., Iran) to prepare core samples for flooding. The gas permeability and porosity of the core samples (Table 1 ) were examined using a gas permeation apparatus (Petro Pazhouhesh Ahoura Co., Iran). Core slabs (with 1-in diameter and 0.5 cm thickness) for wettability alteration studies were obtained using a common method, as described in the literature [37].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the GQDs were obtained using a Philips microscope (EM208S, Netherlands) at 100 kV using the standard method, and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (Shimadzu-8400 s FT-IR spectrophotometer) spectra in the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1 at room temperature were obtained using a potassium bromide pellet. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum was determined under UV light using a NanoDrop 2000 UV–Vis spectrometer in the absorption range of 190–1100 nm. To determine the distribution and size of the NPs, dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Nanotrac Wave II Particle Size Zeta Potential Analyzer) measurement was performed at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFT, contact angle, and viscosity measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of the polymer–NP solution on the IFT between oil and water was evaluated using an IFT-400 tensiometer (Fars EOR Co., Iran). The amount of IFT was determined via the diameter shape analysis method using the following equation [38]:
(1)
I F T =
Δ ρ · g ·
R 0
β
where g, Δρ, and
R 0
are the gravitational constant, density difference between fluids, and drop radius of curvature at the apex, respectively, and
β
is the shape factor, defined by the Young–Laplace equation [35,39].

The contact angle for wettability alteration studies was directly determined using the sessile drop method [40], in which the prepared slabs were washed and then dried at 70 °C. The slabs were then aged in crude oil for three days until they were completely saturated with oil, and their wettability became oil wet. Next, a drop was injected into the slab using a needle, and the created contact angle was captured using a camera and measured using ImageJ software [41].

A sweep shear rate test of the polymer–NP solution was performed using a rheometer (RST-CC Brookfield, USA) at ambient temperature (25 °C) to investigate the rheological properties for a high shear rate—from 500 (1/s) to 1600 (1/s)—owing to the technical limitations of the rheometer used.

Response surface methodology (RSM) is an efficient method for reducing the number of experiments required to evaluate the effects of some factors on the responses. Thus, it can significantly save time and cost, particularly when the number of factors and related responses is high. Moreover, with this technique, the interaction between two factors can be properly evaluated [42,43]. In this study, the factors affecting the related responses, including IFT, contact angle, and viscosity, were selected as the NP concentration (A), polymer concentration (B), and salinity (C) through a sensitivity analysis based on previous studies. Three NP concentrations (0.5, 1, and 1.5 wt%) and four polymer concentrations (0 (fresh water without polymer), 1000, 2000, and 3000 ppm) were considered. Regarding the salinity factor, in addition to the salt concentration, the type of salt, in terms of monovalent and divalent ions, is extremely important [44], therefore, two salt types were selected. The first (low-salinity water) comprised monovalent ions (42000 ppm NaCl) while the second (high-salinity water) comprised monovalent and divalent ions (42000 ppm NaCl + 18000 ppm CaCl2). Table 2 shows the samples that should be prepared using RSM, and the aforementioned tests were performed on these samples.

To increase the accuracy of the results, all experiments were performed three times, and the results were input into the software. Based on the analysis performed in this phase, a polymer–NP solution for flooding was developed as an EOR fluid.

Routine and well-known procedures were followed for core preparation and saturation using a common setup (Fig. 1 ) [45]. First, the core samples were washed with fresh water to remove any impurities and then dried in an oven at 50 °C for 2 h. In the second step, the core samples were flooded with prepared saline water from two ends (forced imbibition) and then aged in saline water for three weeks for spontaneous imbibition to ensure that they were fully saturated with brine. Next, crude oil was flooded into the core samples until no more water was released, followed by merging in oil for two weeks.

Afterward, core samples were prepared for EOR simulations. The first step in the EOR simulation process involves flooding the core samples with saline water until no more oil is ejected. Then, the proposed polymer–NP solution in the Design Expert phase was flooded, followed by saline water flooding as a post-flushing step until extra oil was removed. An injection rate of approximately 0.5 cm3/min (which is close to the front rate in porous media in field-scale flooding operations (1 ft/d) [46] was maintained in all the above steps. At higher injection rates, breakthrough/fingering may occur during core saturation steps, in which case the entire core is not swept; hence, neither water saturation nor oil saturation is performed properly. In addition, if the injection rate is high during the EOR simulation steps, the macroscopic sweep efficiency is greatly reduced, and the recovery will not produce a good result [27,45].

The amount of effluent fluid along with pressure was monitored in each step, and the recovery factor was measured using the following equation [46]:
(2)
R e c o v e r y
F a c t o r
( % )
=
P o r e
v o l u m e
( i n g )
( C u m u l a t i v e
v o l u m e
o f
o i l
p r o d u c e d )
P o r e
v o l u m e ( p o r o u s
m e d i a )
× 100

Fig. 2 displays a TEM image of the fabricated GQDs with a diameter of approximately 10 nm. Here, the GQDs appear as extremely small circular dots. These are placed next to each other in some areas to form larger particles because very small particles shrink at higher temperatures and aggregate to form larger particles [47,48].

The FT-IR spectrum, presented in Fig. 3 , shows the presence of several oxygen functional groups on the graphene surfaces and edges of the quantum dots at approximately 3426.35 cm−1 [49,50]. The wide peak is attributed to the bending vibration of the OH group. Furthermore, the two peaks at 1389.45 and 1567.71 cm−1, assigned to the CC and COO bonds, respectively, confirm the existence of such groups [35,49]. The tensile vibration of COC at 1257 cm−1 is attributed to a cross-link, and the peaks are slightly different [51]. The peaks at 1004.1 and 2928.4 cm−1 correspond to the CO and CH bonds, respectively, confirming the existence of hydroxyl groups. Some weak vibrations are observed at 823 cm−1, which may have resulted from a sharp decrease in H. The presence of oxygenated functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and carbonyl groups, leads to better dissolution of GQDs in water [52].

The UV spectrum consists of an absorption peak at 260 nm in the n–π* group (Fig. 4 ), which represents the carbon transfer of sp2 in the GQD structure in the CO transition group [53,54]. Oxygen and amine-based groups at the level of GQDs are among the factors affecting the optical bandgap and demonstrate the importance of synthesis. In addition, a GQD adsorption band is observed at 366 nm. This is related to the transfer of π–π* and graphene points from the quantum point of the CC group [54,55].

The particle size was measured using DLS. Fig. 5 indicates that the majority of accumulated NPs have sizes between 1 and 10 nm, as shown in the TEM image in accordance with the dot and quantum nature of the particles.

To investigate the rheological behaviors, the shear viscosity and shear stress as functions of shear rate for the designed polymer–NP solutions were investigated via a shear rate sweep test. The shear stress and shear viscosity exhibited similar behavior; increasing the polymer concentration increased the shear viscosity and shear stress with a shear-thickening observed at a narrow range of higher shear rates, realized using our rheometer. Polymer chains were adsorbed on the surface of the NPs and acted as a linker between the polymer chains, leading to the formation of heavy aggregates and the creation of stable macromolecular structures that are not easily broken, thus increasing the shear viscosity and shear stress [56–58]. A similar trend was observed when graphite oxide NPs were added to a polyacrylamide with high molecular weight (as an associative polymer) to improve the shear viscosity at high temperatures and salinity. This compound showed a viscosity stability of 53.7%, which is much better than that of ordinary and expensive polymers. Due to the presence of NP functional groups, the resulting compound is more hydrophilic and better dispersed in electrolyte solutions [59].

The polymer concentration of 3000 ppm significantly increased the viscosity compared with the other concentrations. Divalent salts (CaCl2) increased the shear stress and viscosity at 0.5 and 1.5 wt%, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of NPs with salts and polymers as well as their behavior in viscosity (Figs. 6–8
). At other polymer concentrations, the water-containing monovalent (NaCl) and divalent (CaCl2) salts exhibited approximately the same shear stress and viscosity. As depicted in the following figures, in the low-shear-rate region, the profile shows minimal viscosity because of the minimal effect of the interaction between the polymer chains and NPs. Owing to the limitation in the rheometer employed, the shear-thinning behavior could not be measured at a shear rate lower than 500 s−1, whereas at the high-shear-rate region, the shear viscosity increased, exhibiting shear-thickening characteristics [8,60–63]. When NPs were added at high shear rates, the fluid showed greater shear strength, shear thickness, and viscoelastic behavior because the structures of NPs in the polymer solution were modified, promoting polymer-like behavior [64,65]. Although the change in shear viscosity versus shear rate is low for each unit sample, excessive shear-thickening behavior observed with polymer–NP solutions may be disadvantageous and can result in severe injectivity issues, particularly in low-permeability reservoirs (due to high shear rates at the perforations and in the near-wellbore region [29,66]. Therefore, optimum concentrations of the polymer and NPs should be selected. With the addition of NPs, the polymer also exhibited non-Newtonian cutting-dependent behavior at high pressure [64]. This prevents the Brownian motion of molecules, resulting in the entanglement of polymer molecules and stiffening of the internal structure of the polymer coils, thus increasing the fluid viscosity [58,67–70]. Moreover, the salt concentration did not have a significant effect on reducing the viscosity, but the presence of a low concentration (0.5 wt%) of NPs (Fig. 6) significantly increased the viscosity compared with that at higher NP concentrations. When the NP concentration was increased to 1 wt% (Fig. 7) and 1.5 wt% (Fig. 8), the viscosity decreased. This can be attributed to the physical cross-linking property of NPs: there are fewer interactions between the van der Waals forces and polymer chains [71]. The addition of NPs to the optimal concentration, owing to their screening effect and dispersion properties in liquids, increases the surface interaction to electrostatic repulsion and helps bring the polymer molecules closer together, creating longer polymer filaments that can form an interconnected network [72]. The electrostatic interactions of NPs with long polymer networks occur through hydrogen bonds formed between the polymer and NPs [73].

The NP concentration of 0.5 wt% was superior to other concentrations in improving the viscosity. This is important because the optimal concentration can be achieved with regard to economic and injectivity concerns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection procedure of the proposed sample for EOR
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty-three samples were designed using RSM to determine the effect of the polymer and NP concentrations as well as the salinity on the responses, including IFT, contact angles, and viscosity, and to obtain an optimized EOR fluid. After the experiment (discussed in Section 4), determining the exact effects of the salt and nanofluid as a result of instability, integration, and their synergistic effect may prove very difficult. Therefore, the analysis of variance (ANOVA) technique and p-value were used to determine the effect of each variable and the sample with the best responses.

Fig. 9 shows the results of the IFT tests conducted at 25 °C. The initial amount of oil/deionized water was approximately 16 mN/m, whereas the GQDs combined as oil–water interfaces because of the high level of contact with crude oil at low NP concentrations (0.5 wt%) and increased the level of activity between oil and water. This significantly reduces IFT [45,74]; however, at higher concentrations, GQDs accumulated at the oil/water level as a completely closed layer with high cohesion, thus increasing the IFT. Even at low polymer concentrations (1000 ppm), the IFT increased because of polymer aggregation and adhesion [75]. Increasing the polymer concentration to 3000 ppm leads to an increase in the amount of long polymer chains, resulting in an IFT increase to 3.89 mN/m at 1.5 wt% NP concentration. The IFT tests indicate that 0.5 wt% with an IFT of 1.78 mN/m is the optimal GQD concentration [76].

Oil contact angle measurements (Fig. 10 ) were performed as another important assessment of the effect of NPs on wettability at different concentrations. However, the contact angle depends on the rock material, which can provide feedback on the nanofluid performance in a porous medium. After the slabs were placed in the GQDs, the contact angles of the sandstone surface and the carbonate surface increased from 31 to 113° and from 54 to 127°, respectively. The performance of the GQDs in changing the wettability of carbonate surfaces was maximum at 0.5- wt% NP concentration [77,78]. At higher NP concentrations, the contact angles decreased compared with that at 0.5 wt%, bringing the slabs closer to the initial oil-wet state. Therefore, low NP concentrations have a greater influence on the wettability of surfaces because GQDs at low concentrations can easily penetrate the surface pores, and according to the definition of nonbonding pressure, NPs separate the fluid from the surface of the rock by forming a wedge-shaped film [14,79–81]. The results of the IFT tests agree well with the contact angle results, and salinity had no effect on the contact angle. These results are important because the salinity [82] and polymer concentration [83] can affect the contact angle when no NPs exist in the medium.

The viscosity results for the prepared samples are listed in Table 3 . With increasing polymer concentration in nanofluids, dilute concentrations become semi-dilute and ultimately become concentrated [76,84]. In addition, polymer chains initially interact with solvent molecules and are considered an ideal mixture; however, as the amount of polymer increases, the polymer chains become increasingly strong. This reduces the mobility of polymer molecules relative to dilute solutions and proportionally increases the viscosity [60,73].

A p-value of less than 0.05 when using ANOVA indicates a significant contribution of each factor in the model, whereas the terms AB, BC, and AC represent the interactions between factors. The results in Table 4 indicate that the NP and polymer concentrations had the greatest effect on the IFT. However, regarding the contact angle, only the NP concentration is effective, whereas only the polymer concentration is effective with regard to the viscosity. Moreover, the effect of salinity was insignificant for all the responses. Among the interactions of the factors, only those of the polymer and NPs influenced the contact angle. Therefore, all the polymer–NP solutions were salt-independent and had positive microscopic and macroscopic features because saltwater can be used as a base fluid for the preparation of flooding fluids.
The contact angle results presented in Table 4 are related to carbonate cores because the results obtained for the sandstone surfaces were insignificant in comparison.

From the analysis in Section 2, as well as the ANOVA statistical analyses, the best samples with the highest contact angle, the lowest IFT, and maximum viscosity were chosen (0.5 wt% GQDs and 2000-ppm polymer in the two types of water salinity) as proposed fluids for the flooding step.

The oil recovery factors were 53% and 43% after water flooding and reached 74% and 60% after flooding the proposed fluids into cores B and C, respectively. These oil recovery increments are related to surface characteristic changes with respect to the IFT and wettability in the microscopic efficiency and mobility ratio modification with respect to the viscosity increase in the macroscopic efficiency. In the post-flush water flooding step, oil recovery ended at 84% and 71% in cores B and C, respectively, because the IFT and contact angle were affected for a longer duration.

Both the proposed flooding fluids have good potential for EOR application with respect to the carbonate core. To determine this ability in the sandstone core, a sandstone sample (0.5 wt% GQDs and 2000-ppm polymer in high salinity) was tested. First, in the water flooding step, ΔP increased to 3439 psi during the flooding of 1 PV when the breakthrough occurred. In the next 1-PV flooding, ΔP decreased. However, in the two subsequent flooding steps, ΔP continued to increase until the flooding ended (Fig. 13 ). The recovery factors were approximately 11.6% and 23% after completion of the water flooding and proposed fluid flooding steps, respectively. At the end of the third step, which was post-flush water flooding, ΔP reached 30.3%. From the pressure curve trend, which increases continuously, it can be inferred that the shear viscosity of the flooding fluid is so high that the fluid can be injected properly in such a low permeable sandstone core. The efficiency of the flooding process is lower than that in carbonate cores because of the insignificant contact angle improvement.

To satisfy the demand for energy, EOR methods should be considered in addition to the exploration of new hydrocarbon reservoirs. In this study, high-molecular-weight polymer solutions were used as mediums for dispersing GQDs. Their potential as EOR fluids in carbonate and sandstone cores was investigated through experimental and statistical methods. RSM was utilized in the experiment design, and the IFT, contact angle, and viscosity were selected as the corresponding responses that greatly affect the efficiency of the EOR method. The influencing factors considered were polymer concentration, NP concentration, and salinity. The results demonstrate that the highest reduction in IFT and the greatest increment in contact angle were realized when the fluid containing 0.5 wt% GQDs was used. Increasing the concentration to 1.5- wt% GQDs had an inverse effect on the surface phenomena. Therefore, the concentration of 0.5 wt% was chosen as the optimal GQD concentration. Further, according to the viscosity test results, the polymer concentration of 2000 ppm exhibited the best performance, even under high-salinity conditions, and the synergistic effect of the polymer and NPs compensates for the inverse effect of salt on rheological properties, which is highly beneficial. The results of the experiment were confirmed using ANOVA, and the influencing factors were analytically determined. After these selection procedures were performed, two samples (0.5 wt% GQDs and 2000-ppm polymer) in low- and high-salinity water were proposed for the flooding steps. Both fluids were flooded into the two carbonate cores, but only that with high salinity was flooded into the sandstone core owing to similarity. The corresponding recovery factors increased to 84% and 71% in the carbonate cores and 30.3% in the sandstone core at the end of the third flooding step. Therefore, the high potential of the proposed fluids for EOR was proven with regard to increasing the microscopic sweep efficiency using NPs and increasing the macroscopic sweep efficiency by polymers for both types of core samples, particularly the carbonate type.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Research Foundation of Korea (2021R1A4A2001403).

Shepherd, Controlled hydrophobe branching to match surfactant to crude oil composition for chemical EOR, SPE improved oil recovery symposium, Soc. Pet. Eng. (2012), https://doi.org/10.2118/154084-MS.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selection and evaluation of enhanced oil recovery method using artificial neural network
